digest: Standard-depth audit found practical AI inference carbon reporting is feasible as bounded intensity estimates: self-hosted telemetry can be measured more credibly; managed API reporting remains proxy/modelled due to unverified per-request energy/carbon fields.

finalReport.summary: Working conclusion: for a small SaaS, report AI inference carbon as a bounded software-intensity estimate, not as precise per-request truth or an organization inventory. If using managed AI APIs, start with request/model/token/region/customer logs plus clearly labelled modeled estimates. If self-hosting, instrument GPU/CPU energy and allocate shared/idle infrastructure using an SCI-style boundary and functional unit. Public comparative provider/model claims should be avoided unless methods, boundaries, and functional units are aligned and substantiated.

researchMetadata.expectedFinalShape: bounded research handoff with recommendations, implementation plan, caveats, and claim verdict index
researchMetadata.sourcePolicy: Primary standards, cloud/provider docs, hardware/runtime docs, and reputable methodologies were prioritized; audit-claims verdicts are authoritative.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure expose delayed monthly cloud carbon dashboards/exports/APIs with granularity varying by provider; these are not per-request inference telemetry.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Use provider carbon tools for cloud-level/monthly context, not request-level AI attribution.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: No per-request managed AI energy/carbon telemetry was verified in inspected docs; negative absence was not exhaustively proven across all providers.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Managed API reports must be labelled proxy/modelled, not measured per-request emissions.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Self-hosted inference can measure NVIDIA GPU power/energy via DCGM, CPU package/domain energy via Linux powercap/RAPL, and AMD GPU power/energy via ROCm SMI, within hardware-specific boundaries.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Supports a higher-credibility measured option for self-hosted inference.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Inference energy depends on model/task, hardware, token lengths, batching, KV cache, quantization, utilization, concurrency, and latency; runtime systems expose many relevant logs/metrics.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Defines what the SaaS should log before estimating or optimizing.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: When granular API energy is unavailable, modeled best estimates are allowed if disclosed; CCF-style methods use billing/usage, kWh factors, PUE, grid factors, and embodied emissions, but managed GenAI token/spend proxies remain lower-confidence analogies.
factSlotCoverage.evidenceStatus: verified for general modeled-estimate allowance; partial for managed GenAI-specific factors
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Proxy estimates are acceptable for internal directional metrics if caveated.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Convert energy to operational emissions with O=E×I using kWh and region-specific carbon intensity; include PUE and embodied emissions only if inside the chosen boundary.

Define boundary and functional unit; include reserved/provisioned and significant idle/supporting infrastructure; allocate embodied emissions by time/resource share and active power by measured resource-use proxies where available.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Prevents undercounting shared/idle capacity in SaaS reporting.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Separate organization inventories from product/software intensity: GHG Corporate/Scope 2 for inventories, GHG Product/ISO 14067 for product footprints, SCI for software carbon intensity rates.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Avoids treating request-level AI estimates as formal corporate emissions totals.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Disclose measured vs modeled inputs, point-estimate limitations, boundary choices, location/carbon-factor sensitivity, and scenario ranges where possible.

Synthesis ranking: managed API proxy logs are lowest effort/lowest credibility; self-hosted telemetry is higher effort/higher credibility; hybrid combines provider dashboards, usage logs, and measured self-hosted components.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Useful decision framing, but not a directly sourced benchmark.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Report kWh, kg/gCO2e, gCO2e/request or per 1k tokens, model/provider/region/date, boundary, functional unit, methodology, allocation basis, assumptions, and measured/modelled confidence.
Make comparative carbon claims only with clear, substantiated, fair comparisons and aligned methodology, boundaries, and functional units.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Constrains external marketing and provider/model comparison claims.

mainFindings.finding: Major cloud carbon dashboards are useful for monthly/account/project/resource context but do not solve per-request AI inference attribution.
mainFindings.evidenceStatus: verified/partial for managed AI negative claim
mainFindings.confidence: high for cloud dashboards; medium for absence of managed AI request telemetry

mainFindings.finding: Self-hosted inference can be instrumented with hardware energy telemetry, but readings are scoped to devices/packages/domains rather than automatically representing whole-service emissions.
SCI-style reporting is the best practical framing: define software boundary, functional unit, energy, carbon intensity, embodied allocation, and disclose methodology.
API-only estimates can be modelled, but should be presented as estimates with assumptions and sensitivity, not measured emissions.
mainFindings.evidenceStatus: verified for modeling allowance; partial for GenAI-specific factors

mainFindings.finding: External comparative carbon claims between AI models/providers are risky without aligned methods and substantiation.

recommendations.recommendation: Use a three-tier disclosure: measured self-hosted components, modeled managed-API components, and monthly cloud-provider carbon context. Label each tier separately.
support: Provider carbon dashboards from AWS/GCP/Azure docs

recommendations.recommendation: For managed AI APIs, log provider, model/version, region if known, request timestamp, customer/tenant, prompt tokens, output tokens, latency, cache/batch indicators if available, and cost/usage fields; publish only modeled ranges or internal directional metrics unless provider-specific energy factors are obtained.
recommendations.evidenceStatus: partial for lack of provider telemetry; verified for variables and disclosure principles
support: vLLM metrics docs for analogous runtime fields
audited partial finding that inspected managed API docs did not verify per-request carbon fields

recommendations.recommendation: For self-hosted inference, collect GPU/CPU energy telemetry and request/runtime metrics, then allocate energy to a functional unit such as request or 1k tokens with explicit treatment of idle/reserved capacity.
Do not market a provider or model as greener unless the comparison uses identical or clearly aligned boundaries, functional units, and methods and is legally reviewable.

Dashboard fields should include kWh, gCO2e, gCO2e/request or per 1k tokens, model/provider/region/date, measured-vs-modeled status, boundary, allocation basis, carbon factor source/date, assumptions, and uncertainty/range.

actionPlan.action: Choose the reporting boundary and functional unit before collecting numbers, e.g. inference service per request or per 1k tokens.
actionPlan.source: SCI software boundary and functional-unit guidance
actionPlan.decisionImpact: Prevents later metrics from mixing incompatible scopes.

actionPlan.action: Implement usage logging for all inference paths: provider/model, token counts, latency, tenant/customer, region/location where available, and request outcome.
actionPlan.source: Runtime metrics docs and AI inference energy factors evidence
actionPlan.decisionImpact: Creates the denominator and attribution keys needed for any estimate.

actionPlan.action: If self-hosted, add hardware telemetry collectors for GPU/CPU energy and align samples with request/runtime logs.
actionPlan.source: NVIDIA DCGM, Linux powercap/RAPL, AMD ROCm SMI docs
actionPlan.decisionImpact: Improves credibility from proxy to measured energy for covered hardware.

actionPlan.action: If using managed APIs, build a proxy calculator with explicit modeled status and scenario ranges; do not claim measured per-request CO2e.
actionPlan.source: SCI modeled best-estimate allowance and audit caveat on managed API telemetry absence
actionPlan.decisionImpact: Allows internal tracking while controlling green-claim risk.

actionPlan.action: For external reporting, include a methodology note and avoid cross-provider/model comparisons unless legal/standards criteria are met.
actionPlan.source: SCI, UK Green Claims Code, FTC Green Guides

caveatedFindings.finding: Major managed AI APIs did not show verified per-request energy/kWh/CO2e fields in inspected primary docs, but this is a negative claim and was not exhaustively proven across all schemas/providers.
caveatedFindings.whyItMatters: It determines whether managed API carbon can be measured directly or only estimated.

caveatedFindings.finding: Small-SaaS effort/credibility ranking is a synthesis, not a directly sourced benchmark.
caveatedFindings.whyItMatters: The parent should adapt it to team size, hosting model, compliance needs, and customer expectations.

caveatedFindings.finding: GPU per-workload attribution in Kubernetes/Kepler evidence includes draft/experimental GPU proposal material.
caveatedFindings.whyItMatters: Do not present GPU pod/process attribution as mature without local validation.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: Managed API SaaS can start with request/customer/model/region/tokens/latency logs plus modeled SCI-style estimates, but credibility is low for precise per-request claims.
Self-hosted SaaS can gain higher credibility by measuring GPU/CPU/node energy and allocating measured/idle energy over requests or customers, at higher engineering effort.
vLLM exposes concrete metrics useful for an implementation schema, including token, cache, latency, and request metrics.
unverifiedButRelevant.evidenceStatus: preserved detail under broader verified claim

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: Training/lifecycle case studies show location and boundary choices can materially change AI carbon estimates, but exact numeric examples were preserved rather than used as thresholds.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide whether the product needs internal directional metrics, customer-facing disclosure, or formal accounting.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: The evidence supports bounded intensity estimates, but formal inventories/product CFPs require different standards and assurance.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Use SCI-style intensity for feature/request reporting; use GHG/ISO pathways only for formal corporate/product claims.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide managed API versus self-hosted measurement posture.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Managed API is lower effort but likely proxy-only; self-hosted can measure device energy but adds engineering and allocation complexity.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Start with managed API proxy metrics if speed matters; choose self-hosted or hybrid only if higher credibility justifies operational cost.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Resolve whether external comparative claims are required.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Comparisons require aligned methodology and substantiation; otherwise they create green-claim risk.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Avoid public greener-than comparisons in the first version; publish methodology and ranges instead.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Treat missing managed-provider per-request carbon evidence as a blocking gap for precise API per-request claims, but not for internal modeled estimates.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Completeness conflicts with verification strictness: useful proxy tracking can proceed, but precise claims cannot.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Ask providers directly or inspect full API schemas before promising measured managed-API CO2e.

researchScopeCoverage.status: covered with partial managed-AI negative claim
covered with GPU attribution maturity caveat

blocking.gap: No exhaustive proof that all major managed AI APIs lack per-request energy/carbon fields.
blocking.whyItMatters: Blocks any precise statement that managed API inference emissions are directly unavailable everywhere.
blocking.wouldChange: Provider-specific fields or factors would allow stronger managed API reporting and less proxy uncertainty.

blocking.gap: No primary provider per-token or per-request carbon factors were found for closed managed AI APIs.
blocking.whyItMatters: Blocks exact per-token carbon thresholds or hard customer-facing API emissions numbers.
blocking.wouldChange: Audited provider factors would enable more specific calculators.

nonBlocking.gap: Small-SaaS implementation ranking is synthesized from evidence rather than measured cost/effort benchmarks.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: Recommendations should be adapted to local team capacity.

nonBlocking.gap: Specific universal confidence intervals for AI inference estimates were not established.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: Use scenarios/ranges rather than generic numeric error bars.

nonBlocking.gap: Kepler GPU per-process/pod attribution evidence is draft/experimental.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: Validate locally before relying on it for externally reported GPU workload allocation.

claims.support: AWS CCFT supports monthly delayed carbon data by month, Region, service values, CSV export, and deprecation timing.
Google Cloud supports monthly BigQuery export with billing/project/service/location/model/scopes/kgCO2e fields.
claims.caveat: Some project fields may be null; covers selected billing accounts/services.

claims.support: Azure Carbon optimization supports portal/CSV/REST monthly emissions by subscription/resource dimensions.
Inspected primary docs showed usage/product metrics and monthly/resource-level cloud carbon tools, not per-request AI energy/carbon fields.
claims.caveat: Negative API-surface claim has extraction gaps and was not exhaustive.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Use narrower wording: verification did not find primary evidence of per-request AI inference energy/kWh/CO2e fields across inspected docs.

claims.support: NVIDIA DCGM exposes board power watts and total energy consumption mJ fields.
Linux powercap/RAPL exposes energy_uj and max_energy_range_uj and documents no instantaneous power_uw for Intel RAPL example.
ROCm SMI exposes GPU power and energy accumulator; scope is GPU/HBM graphics package.
claims.caveat: Docs imply, rather than explicitly state, not whole-host energy.

claims.support: Studies/runtime docs support key model, hardware, token, batching, cache, utilization, and latency factors.
claims.caveat: No single source proves every factor independently.

claims.support: SCI permits modeled estimates where real data is unavailable; CCF estimates from usage/billing, factors, PUE, grid, embodied emissions.
claims.caveat: Both emphasize limited accuracy when granular data is absent.

claims.support: SCI operational emissions use O=E×I with kWh and region-specific gCO2eq/kWh; market-based measures excluded from grid intensity.
SCI embodied emissions allocation uses TE, time share, and resource share.
SCI requires boundary and functional unit and includes reserved/provisioned hardware and significant supporting/idle infrastructure.
claims.caveat: Functional-unit examples are not mandatory choices.

claims.support: Kepler docs describe active/idle power attribution by CPU-time ratios; GPU attribution proposal is draft/experimental.
claims.caveat: GPU evidence is not stable GA documentation.

claims.support: Standards split corporate inventories, product footprints, and software intensity rates.
claims.caveat: SCI is not a corporate total or product CFP standard.

claims.support: CCF point estimates, SCI modeled estimates, and AI studies support uncertainty/boundary disclosure.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Disclose assumptions, boundaries, methodology, modeled-vs-measured data, and sensitivity rather than exact point estimates.

claims.support: SCI and green-claims guidance require aligned methodology and clear, fair, substantiated, qualified environmental comparisons.
claims.caveat: AI provider/model comparison is an application of general rules.
